before:
In Nigeria, inflation has been a persistent challenge, affecting the purchasing power of citizens. The current inflation rate stands at 21.09% as of February 2023, according to the National Bureau of Statistics. This rise in inflation has significant implications for individuals, businesses, and the economy as a whole. As a Nigerian, it is essential to understand how inflation affects your money and take proactive steps to mitigate its impact.

Understanding Inflation

Inflation is a sustained increase in the general price level of goods and services in an economy over time. In Nigeria, the high inflation rate is attributed to various factors, including the COVID-19 pandemic, insecurity in some parts of the country, and the Russia-Ukraine conflict, which has led to a shortage of food and other essential items. The Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) has implemented various monetary policies to curb inflation, including increasing the Monetary Policy Rate (MPR) to 18%.

Sectoral Impact of Inflation

Inflation affects various sectors of the economy, including: Also read: Seeds of promise.

  • Agriculture: Inflation affects the price of agricultural inputs, such as fertilizers and seeds, making it challenging for farmers to produce at a lower cost. This increase in production costs is passed on to consumers, leading to higher food prices. The government has implemented policies, such as the Anchor Borrowers' Programme, to support farmers and improve food production.
  • Manufacturing: The high cost of raw materials and energy due to inflation increases the production costs of manufacturers, making their products less competitive in the market. The CBN has introduced the ₦1 trillion Manufacturing Intervention Fund to support manufacturers and improve their productivity.
  • Healthcare: Inflation affects the price of medical supplies and equipment, making healthcare services more expensive. The government has implemented policies, such as the National Health Insurance Scheme, to improve access to healthcare services.
  • Education: Inflation increases the cost of educational materials, tuition fees, and other related expenses, making it challenging for parents to provide quality education for their children. The government has introduced the ₦10 billion Education Intervention Fund to support the education sector.

Protecting Your Money from Inflation

To mitigate the impact of inflation on your money, consider the following strategies:

  • Invest in assets that historically perform well during inflationary periods, such as real estate, stocks, or commodities.
  • Take advantage of high-yield savings accounts or fixed deposit accounts that offer higher interest rates than traditional savings accounts.
  • Consider investing in the ₦100 billion Infrastructure Corporation of Nigeria (InfraCorp), which provides an opportunity to invest in critical infrastructure projects.
  • Diversify your investment portfolio to minimize risk and maximize returns.

Government Initiatives to Curb Inflation

The government has introduced several initiatives to curb inflation, including: Also read: Nigeria Funding and Opportunities Briefing: part 2.

  • The ₦5 trillion Presidential Economic Advisory Council (PEAC) stimulus package to support businesses and individuals affected by the pandemic.
  • The ₦1 trillion CBN Intervention Fund to support critical sectors of the economy, such as agriculture, manufacturing, and healthcare.
  • The ₦10 billion BOI Intervention Fund to support small and medium-scale enterprises (SMEs).

To benefit from these initiatives, individuals and businesses can visit the CBN or BOI websites to learn more about the eligibility criteria and application process. Alternatively, they can contact their local banks or financial institutions for more information.

Next Steps

As a Nigerian, it is essential to stay informed about the current economic trends and take proactive steps to protect your money from inflation. Consider the following next steps: Also read: Nigeria Funding & Opportunities Briefing: part 2.

  • Review your budget and adjust your spending habits to account for the rise in inflation.
  • Invest in assets that provide a higher return than the inflation rate.
  • Take advantage of government initiatives and intervention funds to support your business or personal financial goals.
  • Stay informed about the latest economic developments and adjust your financial strategy accordingly.

By taking these steps, you can mitigate the impact of inflation on your money and achieve your financial goals. Visit your local bank or financial institution today to learn more about the available options and start protecting your money from inflation.
